title: Arterial Line Damping, Zeroing and Levelling

content.text: Damping, zeroing and levelling decide whether the arterial pressure number is trustworthy. In CT-ICU, an inaccurate arterial line can drive harmful treatment: unnecessary vasodilators for a falsely high systolic pressure, missed hypotension from overdamping, or inappropriate fluid/vasopressor changes after cardiac surgery.

content.definition: The ability of the arterial monitoring system to reproduce the patient’s real arterial pressure without excessive blunting or ringing.
A sluggish system that blunts the waveform. It underestimates systolic pressure, overestimates diastolic pressure and may make pulse pressure falsely narrow.
An oscillating system that rings after pressure changes. It overestimates systolic pressure, underestimates diastolic pressure and creates a falsely wide pulse pressure.

rows: Flattened upstroke, absent notch, narrow pulse pressure.
Check for clot, air, kink, loose connection, low flush pressure, catheter against wall; flush and square-wave test.
Tall sharp systolic peak, oscillations after flush, wide pulse pressure.
Remove excess tubing, secure limb, remove air, tighten connections, avoid soft/compliant tubing.
All values offset high or low.
Open stopcock to air, close to patient, press zero, then return stopcock to patient monitoring position.
Place transducer at right atrial/phlebostatic level.

content.text: A transducer 10 cm below the right atrium can over-read pressure by about 7.4 mmHg; 10 cm above can under-read by about 7.4 mmHg. This is enough to change vasopressor decisions.

The arterial system is a dynamic pressure system. The catheter and tubing must transmit pressure rapidly and accurately to the transducer. If the system is too compliant, partially blocked or full of air, the pressure wave is absorbed and blunted. If the system resonates, the pressure wave overshoots and rings. Both errors can produce numbers that look precise but are physiologically wrong.

Overdamping is dangerous in CT-ICU because it can hide hypertension and make pulse pressure appear narrow. Underdamping is dangerous because it can exaggerate systolic hypertension after aortic surgery or valve surgery, leading to unnecessary vasodilators or confusion about bleeding risk. MAP is generally less affected than systolic or diastolic pressure, but severe damping errors can distort all values.

content.definition: A dynamic response test where the flush device is briefly activated to create a square wave, then released to observe oscillations. It helps distinguish optimal damping, overdamping and underdamping.

content.text: A good response has a rapid vertical rise, flat plateau, rapid fall and usually 1 to 2 oscillations before returning to baseline. This means the system is fast enough to transmit the waveform without excessive ringing.

An overdamped response has slow rise/fall and little or no oscillation. Causes include clot at catheter tip, air bubbles, kinked tubing, loose connection, blood in tubing, catheter against vessel wall, low pressure bag, soft tubing or long extension tubing.

An underdamped response has excessive oscillations, often more than 2 after the flush. Causes include long tubing, overly stiff or resonant setup, catheter whip, patient movement and poor limb positioning. The displayed systolic pressure can be falsely high by 10-30 mmHg or more in severe cases.

content.definition: The approximate right atrial level, usually the fourth intercostal space at the mid-axillary line in the supine patient. It is the standard reference level for arterial, CVP and PA pressures.

content.text: Hydrostatic pressure changes by approximately 0.74 mmHg per cm of height difference between transducer and the reference level. If the transducer is too low, the column of fluid adds pressure and the reading is falsely high. If it is too high, the reading is falsely low. For a patient sitting up, level to the right atrium, not the mattress or bed frame.

items: Check that the pressure bag is at 300 mmHg and flush solution is connected.
Inspect the entire system from patient to monitor for air, clot, kinks, loose caps, wet connections and excess tubing.
Level the transducer to the phlebostatic axis/right atrium at the patient’s current position.
Zero the transducer with the stopcock open to air and closed to the patient.
Run a fast-flush square-wave test if waveform quality is questionable.
Compare with cuff BP and clinical perfusion if the arterial trace remains doubtful.

content.text: If the waveform is overdamped, systolic pressure may be falsely low. This can make a hypertensive aortic surgery patient look safe when they are not.
If the waveform is underdamped, do not chase the systolic pressure blindly. Check MAP, square-wave response and clinical context.
Levelling errors are common after bed height changes, sitting the patient up, turning, mobilising, imaging transfers and return from theatre.
Zeroing is not the same as levelling. Zeroing calibrates atmospheric pressure; levelling aligns the transducer to the patient’s right atrium. You need both.

items: Use dedicated pressure tubing, not ordinary soft IV extension tubing.
Keep the line as short as practicable and avoid unnecessary three-way taps or extensions.
Prime slowly and remove every visible air bubble because air absorbs pressure and causes damping.
Maintain pressure bag at 300 mmHg to deliver the continuous flush, usually around 3 mL/hr depending on device.
Secure the transducer holder so it moves with patient position changes or is re-levelled after every position change.
Label arterial line clearly and ensure alarms are appropriate for the post-op target.

content.text: Troubleshooting is equipment plus physiology. If the line is technically poor but the patient is unstable, escalate while troubleshooting. Do not spend ten minutes perfecting a trace while a tamponade, bleed or RV failure is evolving.

rows: 65-80 mmHg for most stable post-cardiac surgery patients; 70-90 mmHg often used when renal, cerebral, aortic or graft perfusion is a concern.
Interpret with urine output, lactate, mentation, skin perfusion and surgical target. A MAP of 65 can be inadequate in chronic hypertension or after aortic/neurological risk surgery.
Narrow pulse pressure suggests low stroke volume, tamponade, severe vasoconstriction or RV failure. Very wide pulse pressure suggests low SVR, aortic regurgitation or IABP augmentation.
At least 0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly aim 30 mL/hr minimum in average adult.
Falling urine output with a deteriorating waveform is an early perfusion warning after cardiac surgery.
Usually <2 mmol/L; trend should fall after bypass and resuscitation.
Persistent or rising lactate >3-4 mmol/L suggests ongoing oxygen debt, low cardiac output, vasoplegia, bleeding, sepsis or limb/mesenteric ischaemia.
About 0.74 mmHg per cm height difference
10 cm error equals about 7.4 mmHg BP error.
Maintains continuous flush and prevents blood reflux.
Exaggerated systolic and low diastolic; secure/shorten system and remove air.

quiz.question: A transducer is 10 cm below the phlebostatic axis. What will happen?
options: BP will read falsely high by about 7-8 mmHg
BP will read falsely low by about 7-8 mmHg
quiz.explanation: A transducer below the right atrium adds hydrostatic pressure. Zeroing does not correct levelling error.

quiz.question: A fast flush test shows no oscillation and a slow return to baseline. What is this?
quiz.explanation: A sluggish square wave response with no oscillation indicates overdamping, which blunts systolic pressure and narrows pulse pressure.

quiz.question: Which value is usually least distorted by mild underdamping?
quiz.explanation: MAP is generally less affected than systolic and diastolic values in mild-moderate damping problems, though severe technical faults can affect all readings.
